mappings.rationale: Directly covers China since 1912 including the republic, the interwar years, the war against Japan, the communist revolution, and reform era discussed throughout subsection A.
Directly covers the development of Maoist ideology, the role of peasants, the Cultural Revolution, and Mao's Reconstruction discussed in subsections A.4.d and A.5.c.
Directly covers the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution (1966-76) including attacks on cultural leaders and party members discussed in subsection A.5.c.iii.
Directly covers Japanese history since the early 20th century including the rise of militarism, WWII, postwar democratization, and economic growth discussed in subsection B.
Directly covers Korean history including Japanese rule (1910-45), the Korean War, and the development of both North and South Korea discussed in subsection C.
Relevant to the Chinese Communist Party, Maoist ideology, the People's Republic, and the communist dimension of the Korean conflict discussed throughout subsections A and C.
Relevant to Sun Yat-sen's political ideas, Chiang Kai-shek's idealization of Chinese tradition, and the Confucian cultural heritage discussed in subsection A.4.
Relevant to the cultural dimensions of modern Chinese history including literature for the masses during the Great Leap Forward discussed in subsection A.5.c.i.
Relevant to the cultural developments in Japan including postwar literary and intellectual life discussed in subsection B.1.d.
Provides background on Japanese warrior culture relevant to understanding the rise of militarism and the military's seizure of initiative in foreign policy discussed in subsection B.2.
Relevant to Chinese nationalism (May Fourth Movement), Japanese militarist nationalism, and Korean independence movements discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to Japan's war in China, the Greater East Asia Co-prosperity Sphere, the Pacific war, and Japan's unconditional surrender discussed in subsections A.3 and B.2.
Relevant to the atomic bombing of Japanese cities, China becoming a nuclear power, and the nuclear dimension of the Korean War discussed in subsections A.5.c.ii and B.2.c.
Relevant to the Sino-Soviet split, Chinese rapprochement with the U.S., the Korean War, and Cold War dynamics in East Asia discussed throughout.
Relevant to the Chinese Revolution, the establishment of the People's Republic, and the revolutionary transformations of Chinese society discussed throughout subsection A.
Relevant to the development of Kuomintang and Chinese Communist ideologies, Sun Yat-sen's three principles, and Maoist thought discussed in subsection A.4.
Provides historical context for China's international connections and the geopolitical significance of Central Asian regions relevant to Sino-Soviet relations.
Relevant to Sun Yat-sen's democratic ideals, the pro-democracy movement (1989), and Taiwan's gradual democratization discussed in subsections A.4.b and A.6.
Relevant to the authoritarian regimes discussed including Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government, Mao's communist state, North Korean dictatorship, and initial KMT rule in Taiwan.
Relevant to trade and human-rights disputes between U.S. and China, the forcible repression of the pro-democracy movement (1989), and political repression in North Korea.
Relevant to the complex diplomacy of the region including rapprochement between China and the U.S./Japan (1972), Sino-Soviet friction, and Korean armistice negotiations.
Relevant to the international dimensions of East Asian conflicts including the Korean War, UN representation, and trade disputes.
Relevant to Japan's great economic growth, China's introduction of economic incentives and market reforms, and South Korea's economic development.
Relevant to the economic transformations across East Asia including Japan's rise as a major world trader and China's rapid economic growth.
Relevant to the Chinese Civil War (1945-49), the Korean War, and the internal conflicts discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the Sino-Japanese War, the Pacific War, the Chinese Civil War, and the Korean War discussed throughout.
Relevant to the fire and atomic bombing of Japanese cities and the Allied air campaigns in the Pacific discussed in subsection B.2.c.
Relevant to Japan's loss of colonies, Korean independence, and the end of Western colonial influence in East Asia discussed in subsection B.1.d.
Relevant to the geopolitical rivalries between China, Japan, the U.S., and the Soviet Union that shaped the region discussed throughout.
